quiz_questions.question_text: Ocean waves are generated primarily by:

options.text: Earthquakes
Wind blowing across the water surface
The Moon's gravity
Ocean currents

quiz_questions.explanation: Most ocean waves are generated by wind transferring energy to the water surface through friction, with wave size determined by wind speed, duration, and fetch (the distance over which the wind blows across open water).

quiz_questions.question_text: Tides are caused primarily by:

options.text: Wind patterns over the ocean
The gravitational pull of the Moon and Sun on Earth's water
Underwater volcanic activity
Earth's rotation alone

quiz_questions.explanation: Tides are caused by the gravitational attraction of the Moon (the primary cause) and the Sun acting on Earth's ocean water, creating bulges of water on both the side facing the Moon and the opposite side, resulting in the regular rise and fall of sea level.

quiz_questions.question_text: Spring tides occur when:

options.text: It is springtime (March-May)
The Sun, Moon, and Earth are aligned, producing extra-high and extra-low tides
The Moon is at its closest approach to Earth
Strong winds create higher than normal waves

quiz_questions.explanation: Spring tides occur during new and full moon phases when the Sun, Moon, and Earth are aligned, and the gravitational forces of the Sun and Moon combine to produce the highest high tides and lowest low tides of the lunar cycle.

quiz_questions.question_text: Neap tides occur when:

options.text: The Sun, Moon, and Earth are aligned
The Sun and Moon are at right angles relative to Earth, producing the smallest tidal range
There is no Moon visible in the sky
A hurricane approaches the coast

quiz_questions.explanation: Neap tides occur during the first and third quarter moon phases when the Sun and Moon are at right angles to each other relative to Earth, partially canceling each other's gravitational pull and producing the smallest difference between high and low tides.

quiz_questions.question_text: The wavelength of an ocean wave is the distance between:

options.text: The wave crest and the ocean floor
Two consecutive wave crests
The shore and the wave
The crest and the trough of the same wave

quiz_questions.explanation: Wavelength is the horizontal distance between two successive corresponding points on a wave, most commonly measured from crest to crest (or trough to trough), and it can range from centimeters for ripples to hundreds of meters for ocean swells.

quiz_questions.question_text: As a wave approaches shore, it slows down and:

options.text: Decreases in height
Increases in height and eventually breaks
Disappears completely
Reverses direction

quiz_questions.explanation: As waves enter shallow water, friction with the ocean floor slows the wave base while the crest continues forward, causing the wave to steepen, increase in height, and eventually become unstable and break when the water depth is roughly 1.3 times the wave height.

quiz_questions.question_text: A tsunami is different from a regular ocean wave because it is caused by:

options.text: Very strong winds during hurricanes
The Moon's gravitational pull during spring tides
A sudden displacement of ocean water from an earthquake, volcanic eruption, or landslide
Changes in ocean temperature

quiz_questions.explanation: Tsunamis are generated by sudden, large-scale displacement of ocean water, most commonly from undersea earthquakes that move the seafloor vertically, but also from volcanic eruptions and submarine landslides, producing waves with extremely long wavelengths that can cross entire ocean basins.

quiz_questions.question_text: Most coastlines experience _____ tide cycles per day.

options.text: One (diurnal)
Two (semidiurnal)
Three
Four

quiz_questions.explanation: Most coastlines experience semidiurnal tides with two high tides and two low tides approximately every 24 hours and 50 minutes (matching the lunar day), though some locations have diurnal (one cycle) or mixed tidal patterns depending on geography.

quiz_questions.question_text: Wave height is determined by wind speed, wind duration, and:

options.text: Water temperature
Fetch (the distance over which the wind blows across open water)
Moon phase
Ocean salinity

quiz_questions.explanation: Wave height depends on three factors: wind speed (stronger winds create bigger waves), wind duration (how long the wind blows), and fetch (the uninterrupted distance over which the wind blows across water), with all three needing to be large for the biggest waves.

quiz_questions.question_text: Longshore drift is the movement of sediment along a coast caused by:

options.text: Tidal currents only
Waves approaching the shore at an angle
Wind blowing sand along the beach
River sediment being deposited at the coast

quiz_questions.explanation: Longshore drift occurs when waves approach the shore at an angle, pushing sediment up the beach diagonally with the swash, while gravity pulls it straight back down with the backwash, creating a zigzag movement that transports sediment along the coast.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the tidal range?

options.text: The distance between the coast and the point where tides begin to form
The vertical difference in water level between high tide and low tide at a given location
The horizontal distance water moves up the beach during high tide
The time between successive high tides

quiz_questions.explanation: The tidal range is the vertical difference between the highest water level at high tide and the lowest water level at low tide, varying from less than 1 meter in some locations to over 15 meters in places like the Bay of Fundy in Canada.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is wave refraction?

options.text: The splitting of a wave into multiple smaller waves
The bending of waves as they approach shore at an angle, caused by the part of the wave in shallower water slowing down first
The reflection of waves off a seawall back into the ocean
The change in wave color as light passes through the water

quiz_questions.explanation: Wave refraction occurs as waves approach a coastline at an angle and the portion in shallower water slows down while the deeper portion continues at its original speed, causing the wave crest to bend and become more parallel to the shore.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a rip current?

options.text: A current that flows along the shore parallel to the beach
A narrow, fast-moving channel of water flowing from the shore back out to sea through the surf zone
A deep ocean current caused by thermohaline circulation
A tidal bore that rushes up a river

quiz_questions.explanation: A rip current is a narrow, powerful flow of water moving away from the beach through the surf zone, formed when water pushed ashore by waves finds a channel to flow back out, and it is the leading cause of lifeguard rescues at beaches.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is fetch in the context of wave formation?

options.text: The depth of water beneath a wave
The uninterrupted distance of open water over which wind blows to generate waves
The height of a wave from trough to crest
The time between successive wave crests passing a fixed point

quiz_questions.explanation: Fetch is the continuous distance of open water over which wind blows in a consistent direction, and longer fetch allows the wind to transfer more energy to the water surface, generating larger waves, which is why oceans produce bigger waves than lakes.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a tidal bore?

options.text: A hole drilled into the seabed to study tidal patterns
A wall of water that rushes up a river or narrow bay as the tide comes in
The calm period between high tide and low tide
An extremely low tide that exposes the ocean floor

quiz_questions.explanation: A tidal bore is a wave that forms when the leading edge of an incoming tide enters a narrow, shallow river or bay, creating a visible wall of water that travels upstream against the river's current, observed in places like the Amazon, Severn, and Qiantang rivers.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the wave period?

options.text: The total time that waves are active during a storm
The time it takes for two consecutive wave crests to pass a fixed point
The length of time between spring and neap tides
The era in geological history when waves first formed

quiz_questions.explanation: Wave period is the time interval between two successive wave crests (or troughs) passing a fixed point, typically measured in seconds, with longer periods indicating more powerful swells that have traveled greater distances from their generation area.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is coastal erosion?

options.text: The deposition of sand and sediment to build up coastal land
The wearing away and removal of coastal land and sediment by wave action, currents, and weathering
The growth of coral reefs along coastlines
The flooding of coastal areas during high tide

quiz_questions.explanation: Coastal erosion is the gradual removal of land material by wave energy, currents, tides, and weathering, which can be a natural process but is being accelerated in many areas by sea level rise, increased storm intensity, and human modifications to coastlines.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the difference between a wave's crest and trough?

options.text: The crest and trough are different names for the same part of a wave
The crest is the highest point of a wave and the trough is the lowest point
The crest is the front of the wave and the trough is the back
The crest only occurs during high tide and the trough during low tide

quiz_questions.explanation: The crest is the highest point of a wave above the still-water level, and the trough is the lowest point below it, with the vertical distance between crest and trough defining the wave height.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a swell?

options.text: A sudden increase in water level from a storm surge
Long-period, well-organized ocean waves that have traveled far from the storm that generated them
Waves created by an earthquake on the ocean floor
The rising of the tide during a spring tide

quiz_questions.explanation: Swell refers to mature, well-organized waves that have traveled beyond the area where they were generated by a storm, developing a uniform shape and long period as shorter, choppier waves dissipate during the journey across open ocean.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is tidal energy?

options.text: Energy stored in ocean waves generated by wind
Renewable energy harnessed from the predictable rise and fall of tides to generate electricity
The energy released during a tsunami
Thermal energy from warm ocean surface water

quiz_questions.explanation: Tidal energy is a form of renewable energy that captures the kinetic energy of tidal flows or the potential energy of tidal height differences to generate electricity, using technologies like tidal barrages, tidal turbines, or tidal lagoons.

quiz_questions.question_text: A surfer in Hawaii notices that waves arriving from a distant storm in the North Pacific are large and evenly spaced. These waves have traveled thousands of kilometers and are called swell. Compared to locally wind-generated waves, swell waves are:

options.text: Shorter, choppier, and less organized
Longer in period, more uniform and organized, having sorted themselves during their long journey across the ocean
Identical to locally generated waves in all respects
Only visible during spring tides

quiz_questions.explanation: As waves travel away from the storm that generated them, they sort by period (longer waves travel faster) and lose their chaotic, choppy character, arriving as well-organized swell with uniform period and direction, ideal for surfing.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Bay of Fundy in Canada has the world's highest tides, with a tidal range exceeding 15 meters. This extreme range is primarily due to:

options.text: The bay being closer to the Moon than other locations
The bay's funnel shape and length, which amplify the tidal wave through resonance as water is funneled into an increasingly narrow space
Stronger winds in the bay pushing water higher
Underground springs that add extra water during high tide

quiz_questions.explanation: The Bay of Fundy's extreme tides result from tidal resonance: the bay's length happens to match the natural oscillation period of the tidal wave, and its funnel shape forces the incoming water into an increasingly narrow and shallow space, amplifying the tide dramatically.

quiz_questions.question_text: A swimmer caught in a rip current at the beach should:

options.text: Swim directly against the current toward shore
Swim parallel to the shore to escape the narrow rip current, then swim diagonally back to shore once out of it
Dive underwater where the current cannot reach them
Stop swimming and let the current carry them as far out as possible

quiz_questions.explanation: Rip currents are narrow (typically 15-30 meters wide), so the safest response is to swim parallel to shore to escape the rip's pull, then swim at an angle back to shore, rather than exhausting yourself fighting directly against the strong outward current.

quiz_questions.question_text: Coastal engineers build groins (walls perpendicular to the shore) to combat beach erosion. However, this can create problems because:

options.text: Groins attract more storm waves, increasing erosion
While groins trap sediment on the updrift side, they starve the downdrift side of sand, often worsening erosion further along the coast
Groins prevent tides from reaching the beach, killing marine life
Groins cause the ocean floor to sink, creating deeper water near shore

quiz_questions.explanation: Groins interrupt longshore drift, accumulating sand on the updrift side but depriving the downdrift side of its natural sand supply, often causing accelerated erosion downstream and creating a chain reaction that requires more groins along the coast.

quiz_questions.question_text: A coastal community is considering building a tidal energy facility. The most important factor in selecting the site would be:

options.text: Proximity to a large city for easy power distribution
A location with a large tidal range and strong tidal currents to maximize energy generation
The warmest water temperature possible for efficient turbine operation
A location with the fewest waves to protect the equipment

quiz_questions.explanation: Tidal energy generation requires locations with large tidal ranges (typically 5+ meters) and strong tidal currents to provide sufficient water flow to drive turbines efficiently, making sites like narrow channels, bays, and estuaries with large tidal ranges ideal.

quiz_questions.question_text: The 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami killed over 230,000 people across multiple countries. A key lesson learned was that:

options.text: Tsunamis can only affect countries directly adjacent to the earthquake
An early warning system with rapid earthquake detection, ocean sensors, and public alert mechanisms could have saved thousands of lives by providing evacuation time
Building seawalls along all coastlines is the only way to prevent tsunami damage
Tsunamis are completely unpredictable and nothing can be done to prepare

quiz_questions.explanation: The Indian Ocean had no tsunami warning system in 2004, and since tsunami waves can take minutes to hours to reach distant coastlines after an earthquake, an early warning system could have provided critical evacuation time, prompting the establishment of the Indian Ocean Tsunami Warning System.

quiz_questions.question_text: Sea level rise from climate change will increase the impact of storm waves and tides on coastal communities because:

options.text: Higher sea levels mean waves will be generated by stronger winds
Higher baseline sea levels allow waves and storm surge to reach further inland, flooding areas that were previously above the high-water mark
Rising seas will reduce tidal range, concentrating wave energy
Warmer water produces smaller waves that penetrate further inland

quiz_questions.explanation: Even modest sea level rise raises the starting elevation for waves, tides, and storm surge, meaning coastal flooding that previously occurred only during extreme events becomes more frequent, and extreme events can push water further inland than ever before.

quiz_questions.question_text: A marine biologist studying tide pools notices that organisms in the intertidal zone must be adapted to survive dramatic changes between high and low tide. The greatest challenge for intertidal organisms is:

options.text: Constant submersion in deep water
Alternating between being underwater (wave exposure, predators) and being exposed to air (desiccation, temperature extremes, UV radiation)
Lack of nutrients in the tidal zone
Constant darkness from deep water covering the tide pools

quiz_questions.explanation: Intertidal organisms face extreme environmental swings: they must withstand wave energy and aquatic predators during high tide, then survive drying out, temperature extremes, and UV exposure during low tide, making the intertidal zone one of the most demanding habitats on Earth.

quiz_questions.question_text: A tsunami traveling across the deep open ocean has a wavelength of 200 km and moves at approximately 800 km/h, yet ships at sea barely notice it passing. This is because:

options.text: Tsunamis only become dangerous when they reach shallow water near shore
In deep water, the tsunami's enormous wavelength and high speed spread its energy over such a vast area that the wave height is less than 1 meter, undetectable to ships
Ship radars deflect the tsunami wave around the vessel
Tsunamis travel along the ocean floor and do not affect the surface in deep water

quiz_questions.explanation: In deep ocean, a tsunami's energy is distributed over its immense wavelength (often 100-200 km) and the full depth of the water column, producing a wave height of less than 1 meter that is imperceptible to ships, but as it enters shallow water, the energy compresses and wave height can increase to 10+ meters.

quiz_questions.question_text: A property owner on a barrier island notices their beach is shrinking despite no major storms. The most likely long-term cause of this gradual beach loss is:

options.text: Fish eating the sand particles along the shoreline
A combination of longshore drift transporting sand away, sea level rise pushing the shoreline landward, and possibly human interference with natural sediment supply
The Moon pulling sand off the beach during high tide
Underground rivers dissolving the sand from below

quiz_questions.explanation: Gradual beach loss is typically caused by a combination of longshore drift removing more sediment than is replaced, sea level rise that shifts the equilibrium shoreline position landward, and human activities like damming rivers or building jetties that interrupt natural sand supply to the coast.
